conditions: Crohn's disease (diagnosed within the past year)
medications: Naproxen as-needed for pain (used cautiously due to GI symptoms)
Prednisone short tapers during flares (past year)
health.mental_health: Since the Crohn’s diagnosis last year, Taylor has periods of health anxiety and low mood around flare unpredictability, balanced by structured coping strategies from therapy. They are vigilant about relapse triggers related to eating-disorder recovery, especially during appetite loss or restrictive “safe food” spirals when symptoms worsen.
recent_visits: Gastroenterology follow-up at Mount Sinai Hospital IBD clinic (medication adjustment and lab review)
Colonoscopy and biopsies confirming Crohn’s activity (within the past year)
Dietitian sessions focused on Crohn’s nutrition while supporting eating-disorder recovery
Neurology consult at Toronto Western Hospital for migraine management
Family doctor appointment for ADHD medication monitoring and bloodwork (CBC/ferritin)

finance.debt: Carries ~CAD $18,000 in student loans and ~CAD $4,500 on a line of credit used for out-of-pocket therapy, dietitian visits, and a gap in coverage for some Crohn’s-related prescriptions and supplements.
investments: TFSA index ETFs (monthly contributions when symptoms allow consistent budgeting)
High-interest savings account (emergency fund rebuilding after medical expenses)
recent_transactions: CAD $240: psychotherapy session package co-pay (not fully covered)
CAD $78: mesalamine prescription co-pay at Shoppers Drug Mart
CAD $1,150: rent transfer (room in a shared apartment near the Bloor-Danforth line)
CAD $320: lab fees and travel/parking for specialist appointment
CAD $95: grocery delivery during a flare week

relationships: Partner: Samir Patel (they/them), supportive but sometimes frustrated by last-minute cancellations during flares
Best friend: Jenna Kwan, a nurse who helps Taylor reality-check symptoms vs. anxiety spirals
Sibling: Alex Wu (older brother in Vancouver) who checks in weekly and sends care packages
IBD peer-support group contact: “Maya R.” (met through a Toronto support group; mostly text-based)
Work friend: Claire O’Donnell, covers meetings when Taylor has migraine days (with limited disclosure)
social.social_media_activity: Mostly lurks on urbanism Twitter/Bluesky and posts occasional TTC/streetscape photos on Instagram. Uses close-friends stories sparingly for health updates; keeps Crohn’s and eating-disorder recovery off public posts to avoid workplace and extended-family visibility.
private_conversations: Discussing bowel urgency, accidents fears, and how it affects dating/intimacy
Talking with therapist about relapse warning signs for the eating disorder during symptom-driven food restriction
Requesting workplace accommodations without disclosing full diagnosis details
Debating whether to start a stronger Crohn’s medication if symptoms don’t stabilize
Disclosing ADHD medication concerns (appetite suppression) to their prescriber

schedule.work_hours: Typically Mon–Fri, 9:00am–5:30pm with occasional evening public consultations (1–2/month). Hybrid: 3 days in-office, 2 days remote when possible.
regular_appointments: Biweekly psychotherapy (evenings) focused on chronic illness adjustment and eating-disorder recovery
Quarterly gastroenterology follow-up (more frequent during flares)
ADHD medication monitoring with family doctor every 3–4 months
Migraine tracking/check-in as needed (usually every 6 months)
upcoming_events: Public open house for a Complete Streets corridor proposal (next Thursday, 6:30–8:30pm)
IBD clinic bloodwork and stool test drop-off (next week, morning)
Weekend visit with Samir’s family in Mississauga (tentative, depends on symptoms)
Physio intro appointment for posture/neck tension that can trigger migraines (in two weeks)
schedule.daily_routine: Mornings start with meds, a small breakfast planned to avoid migraine triggers, and reviewing a task list app to manage ADHD. Commutes by TTC when in-office; keeps an emergency kit (anti-nausea meds, electrolyte packets, spare clothing) for Crohn’s unpredictability. Evenings are low-key: short walk when well, meal prep on good days, and early bedtime during flares.

professional.company: City of Toronto — Transportation Planning (municipal government)
projects: Complete Streets design review for a midtown corridor (lane reallocation, safety audits)
Development application analysis (shadow studies, community benefits notes, zoning compliance)
Ridership and accessibility memo supporting a bus priority pilot
Community consultation summary reports and council briefing notes
professional.performance_issues: Generally strong work quality, but in the past year has had intermittent sick days and occasional delayed deliverables during Crohn’s flares and migraine clusters. They’ve requested more predictable meeting schedules and increased remote days; worries about being perceived as unreliable and keeps diagnosis details limited to HR.
workplace_relationships: Manager: Priya Desai, supportive about flexible work but expects proactive communication
Senior planner mentor: Mark Hargreaves, values Taylor’s analysis but can be blunt about deadlines
HR accommodations contact: L. Chen (confidential medical documentation on file)
Cross-department collaborator: TTC service planning analyst who depends on Taylor’s data pulls

hobbies: Street photography (especially transit and public space)
Cycling on the Martin Goodman Trail when symptoms allow
Cooking simple, low-fuss meals and experimenting with gut-friendly recipes
Board games with a small friend group (quiet, scheduled hangouts)
Reading urban policy newsletters and long-form journalism
habits: Tracks migraines and GI symptoms in a private app to spot triggers (highly personal notes)
Keeps a “flare kit” in their backpack (wipes, spare underwear, meds, electrolyte packets)
Uses body-doubling/co-working sessions online to manage ADHD task initiation
Limits alcohol and caffeine due to migraines and GI symptoms
Practices meal regularity as part of eating-disorder recovery, but struggles during nausea/flares
Occasional cannabis edible (low dose) for sleep/pain during bad symptom weeks
lifestyle.living_situation: Rents a room in a shared two-bedroom apartment near Koreatown with one roommate; chose the area for TTC access and proximity to clinics. Keeps the bathroom stocked with personal medical supplies and prefers housing stability over upgrading, given unpredictable health costs.
lifestyle.dietary_preferences: Flexible but symptom-informed: avoids known migraine triggers (red wine, aged cheeses) and adjusts fiber/spice during Crohn’s flares. Works with a dietitian to avoid restrictive patterns that could undermine eating-disorder recovery; aims for consistent meals and gentle snacks when appetite is low.
